Implement the following plan:

# Plan: create-amytis CLI (Model A)

## Context

Users want to start a new Amytis-based site without forking/cloning the repo manually.
The CLI (`bunx create-amytis my-blog`) will scaffold a full Amytis project by downloading
the latest GitHub release tarball, then asking two setup questions (site title, description)
before patching `site.config.ts` and running `bun install`.

Model A: user gets the full source — `src/`, `scripts/`, `content/`, `site.config.ts`.
They own all the code; only `content/` and `site.config.ts` need to be touched day-to-day.

---

## Package structure

```
packages/
  create-amytis/
    package.json      ← name: "create-amytis", bin: "dist/index.js"
    src/
      index.ts        ← CLI entry point
    dist/             ← compiled output (gitignored, published to npm)
    tsconfig.json
    .gitignore
```

Root `package.json` gets a bun workspace entry:
```json
"workspaces": ["packages/*"]
```

---

## CLI flow

```
bunx create-amytis [project-name]
```

1. Read `project-name` from `process.argv[2]`; if missing, prompt for it
2. Call GitHub API to get latest release tag:
   `GET https://api.github.com/repos/hutusi/amytis/releases/latest`
3. Download tarball:
   `https://github.com/hutusi/amytis/archive/refs/tags/{tag}.tar.gz`
4. Extract into `./{project-name}/`  (tarball root dir is `amytis-{version}/`, rename it)
5. Prompt for **Site title** (default: project-name)
6. Prompt for **Site description** (default: "My digital garden")
7. Patch `site.config.ts`:
   - Replace `title: { en: "Amytis", zh: "Amytis" }` with user title
   - Replace `description: { en: "...", zh: "..." }` with user description
8. Patch `package.json` in scaffolded dir:
   - Set `"name"` to project-name
   - Set `"private": true` (they're not publishing this)
   - Remove `"repository"`, `"bugs"`, `"homepage"` fields
9. Run `bun install` in the new directory
10. Print success + next steps

---

## Critical files to create

### `packages/create-amytis/package.json`
```json
{
  "name": "create-amytis",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "Create a new Amytis digital garden",
  "license": "MIT",
  "bin": { "create-amytis": "./dist/index.js" },
  "scripts": {
    "build": "bun build src/index.ts --outfile dist/index.js --target node --sourcemap=none",
    "dev": "bun src/index.ts"
  },
  "files": ["dist"]
}
```

### `packages/create-amytis/src/index.ts`

Key implementation details:
- `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang
- Zero npm dependencies — use Node.js built-ins only:
  - `https` for API + tarball download
  - `zlib` (gunzip) + `tar` via child_process (`tar xz`) for extraction
  - `readline` for interactive prompts
  - `fs`, `path`, `child_process` for file ops and install
- Helper `prompt(question, defaultVal)` → `Promise<string>` via readline
- Helper `fetchJson(url)` → GitHub API call with `User-Agent` header (required by GitHub API)
- Helper `downloadTarball(url, dest)` → streams tarball to disk
- Helper `extractTarball(tarPath, targetDir)` → runs `tar xzf` via child_process
- Patch functions use `fs.readFileSync` + regex replace + `fs.writeFileSync`

### `packages/create-amytis/tsconfig.json`
Standard Node.js tsconfig (target ES2022, module NodeNext).

---

## Files to modify

- `package.json` (root): add `"workspaces": ["packages/*"]`

---

## site.config.ts patch strategy

The file has these exact strings to replace:

```ts
title: { en: "Amytis", zh: "Amytis" },
```
→
```ts
title: { en: "My Blog", zh: "My Blog" },
```

```ts
description: { en: "Amytis — an elegant open-source framework...", zh: "Amytis — 优雅的开源数字花园框架。" },
```
→
```ts
description: { en: "My description", zh: "My description" },
```

Use regex with enough context to avoid false matches. The zh value gets the same
user-provided string (user can change it later). The `footerText` field references
"Amytis" by name — also replace those occurrences.

---

## Verification

1. `cd packages/create-amytis && bun run build` — should produce `dist/index.js`
2. `node dist/index.js test-project` — should scaffold `../../test-project/`
3. Check that `test-project/site.config.ts` has the patched title/description
4. Check that `test-project/package.json` has `"name": "test-project"` and `"private": true`
5. Check that `test-project/node_modules` exists after install

---

## Out of scope (follow-up)

- GitHub Actions workflow for publishing `create-amytis` to npm
- Cleaning up example `content/` posts during scaffold
- Non-bun install fallback (npm/yarn detection)


If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/hutusi/.claude/projects/-Users-hutusi-workspace-ai-naive-amytis-claude-review/3ad163be-6e63-499f-81de-46ed77ebd353.jsonl

    "remark-gfm": "^4.0.1",
    "remark-math": "^6.0.0",
    "unist-util-visit": "^5.1.0",
    "zod": "^4.3.6"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@tailwindcss/postcss": "^4.1.18",
    "@types/bun": "^1.3.9",
    "@types/d3": "^7.4.3",

content.thinking:
Now I can see the footer code. Let me analyze the mobile issues:

1. **Grid layout** - `grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-4 gap-12 mb-12` - On mobile, the grid stacks to 1 column. The Brand section is `lg:col-span-2`, then Navigation and Connect are each 1 column. On mobile this results in 3 stacked sections with `gap-12` (48px) between them - that's quite a lot of vertical spacing.

2. **Bottom bar** - `flex flex-col md:flex-row justify-between items-center gap-4 text-xs` - On mobile it stacks vertically. The `LanguageSwitch`, a `|` separator, a Privacy link, another `|` separator, and the Built With link are all in a horizontal `flex` with `gap-6` - on mobile this horizontal row could get cramped. The `|` separators could look odd if the row wraps.
   
The vertical padding of 64px is quite generous for mobile and makes the footer unnecessarily tall, while the 48px gap between grid sections compounds the spacing issue. On narrow screens, the bottom bar's horizontal flex with the language switcher, separators, and links could feel cramped or wrap awkwardly. The real bottleneck on mobile is the footer's total height—with the padding and gaps stacked together, it could easily exceed 500px. I'd suggest reducing the vertical spacing on smaller screens by using responsive classes like `gap-8 lg:gap-12` and `py-10 lg:py-16`, and potentially laying out the "Explore" and "Connect" sections side-by-side on mobile to compress the vertical footprint.

Looking at the actual grid structure, I could shift to a two-column layout at the tablet breakpoint using `grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-4`, keeping the brand section full-width while letting the other sections sit adjacent to each other. On mobile though, everything would still need to stack vertically to avoid cramping the content.

For the bottom bar, I'd add flex wrapping to the links row so they break naturally on smaller screens rather than forcing everything into a single line, and adjust the gap spacing to be tighter on mobile.

content.text:
A few concrete issues I can see:

1. **Excessive vertical padding on mobile** — `py-16` (64px top+bottom) applies at all sizes. Combined with three stacked sections and `gap-12` (48px) between them, the footer can be 500px+ tall on a phone.

2. **Navigation and Connect stack individually** — the grid goes to `grid-cols-1` at mobile, so Brand → Navigation → Connect are three separate full-width blocks. A `grid-cols-2` at `sm` would let Navigation and Connect sit side-by-side (which is the natural reading of two short link lists).

3. **Bottom bar pipe separators could wrap awkwardly** — the `flex items-center gap-6` row (LanguageSwitch | Privacy | Built With) has no `flex-wrap`, so on very narrow phones it can overflow or force the parent `flex-col` awkwardly.

Now I have the full picture. Two concrete issues:

1. **Missing `sizes` on `ExportedImage`** — without it the browser treats the image as `100vw` wide regardless of display size, so mobile always fetches the full-resolution variant. The article is `max-w-3xl` (768px) on desktop and full-width on mobile — `sizes` should reflect that.

2. **No overflow containment at the prose level** — the prose wrapper has `max-w-none` (relying on the ancestor `max-w-3xl` article to constrain width). If anything breaks that assumption (books, flows, or an edge-case image), there's no safety net to stop horizontal bleed. Adding `overflow-x-hidden` to the prose `<div>` is a cheap guard.

The `max-w-full h-auto` on both image paths is correct and does constrain display width — the visual problem comes from the browser loading a needlessly large image anyway (because of the missing `sizes`), and the lack of an overflow clip if things go wrong.

```tsx
  unoptimized={isDev || isExternal}
/>

// prose wrapper: add overflow-x-hidden
<div className="prose prose-lg max-w-none overflow-x-hidden ...">
```
